skill.name: mcp-builder
skill.description: Guides developers through designing, implementing, and evaluating MCP servers for external services. Includes TypeScript, Python, security, transport, tool design, and evaluation guidance.
skill.summary: Build MCP servers with practical TypeScript, Python, security, and evaluation guidance.
skill.author: YYH211

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical combined pattern and many high findings, but most are false positives from markdown examples, URLs, API key placeholders, and MCP documentation text. The real risk is the included evaluation harness, which can launch user-specified stdio commands, connect to supplied HTTP or SSE endpoints, pass headers and environment variables, call Anthropic, and write reports. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, so publication is acceptable with a warning.

medium_findings.title: User-Specified MCP Server Execution
medium_findings.description: The evaluation script accepts a stdio command and arguments, then creates a stdio MCP connection that can run that command. This is expected for an MCP evaluator, but users must treat the target server command as trusted code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command and args are explicit CLI inputs that are passed into StdioServerParameters. This confirms executable behavior, but it is core evaluator functionality rather than hidden malicious code.

medium_findings.title: Remote Endpoint and Header Forwarding
medium_findings.description: The evaluation harness accepts user-supplied SSE or HTTP URLs and headers, then connects to those endpoints. This can expose bearer tokens or evaluation traffic to the chosen endpoint if the user supplies an untrusted URL.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The CLI and connection classes clearly support remote URLs and custom headers. The risk depends on user choice of endpoint, so the finding is moderate rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling for Evaluations
medium_findings.description: The documentation and script support API keys, GitHub tokens, authorization headers, and environment variables for evaluation runs. This is legitimate for testing authenticated MCP servers, but secrets can be exposed to local subprocesses or remote endpoints selected by the user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Secret-related inputs are documented and parsed directly by the harness. I found no hardcoded real secrets or forced exfiltration, so this remains a controlled-use risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Trigger Static Command Findings
low_findings.description: Many external command findings come from fenced markdown examples and instructional command snippets. These examples are visible documentation, not code that runs when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation examples, not executable script entry points. They can lead users to run commands manually, but they are not hidden execution paths.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The high weak cryptography hits appear to be caused by documentation text, markdown syntax, and MCP-related terms rather than implemented cryptographic routines. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or custom weak cryptography used by the executable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported lines are headings or guide text rather than crypto implementations. I found no executable cryptographic use in the scripts during contextual review.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Public Documentation References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to MCP documentation, SDK README files, or placeholder endpoints used in examples. They do not by themselves indicate covert network communication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in user-facing instructions and examples. They require user action or user-supplied endpoints, so this is not evidence of concealed callback behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Launch via MCP stdio Transport
dangerous_patterns.description: The evaluator can start a local MCP server process from user-provided command arguments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code passes command, args, and env into stdio_client parameters. It is expected behavior for local MCP evaluation but should be disclosed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote MCP Connection Support
dangerous_patterns.description: The evaluator can connect to user-supplied SSE and streamable HTTP MCP endpoints with optional headers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The SSE and HTTP connection classes directly use supplied URL and header values. The pattern is legitimate but expands network exposure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Report File Write Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The evaluator writes reports to a user-specified output path when the output option is provided.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and user-controlled. It is a low-impact filesystem behavior unless the user chooses a sensitive destination.

content.user_title: Build MCP Servers With Guided Patterns
content.value_statement: MCP server projects often fail because tools are hard for agents to discover, test, or trust. This skill gives structured guidance for design, implementation, security review, and evaluation.
actual_capabilities: Guides MCP server planning, tool naming, transport selection, and API coverage decisions.
Provides TypeScript MCP SDK patterns for server setup, Zod schemas, and tool registration.
Provides Python FastMCP patterns with Pydantic validation and tool annotations.
Includes evaluation guidance for creating realistic XML question and answer test sets.
Includes an evaluation harness for stdio, SSE, and streamable HTTP MCP servers.
Summarizes security practices for authentication, input validation, errors, and DNS rebinding.
limitations: It is a guide and helper toolkit, not a complete MCP server generator.
The evaluation scripts require local dependencies and an Anthropic API key.
Examples use placeholder services and must be adapted before production use.
Users must review any generated MCP server for service-specific security risks.

use_cases.title: Create a New Service Connector
use_cases.description: Plan and implement an MCP server that exposes a third-party service through clear agent tools.

use_cases.title: Standardize Internal MCP Patterns
use_cases.description: Define naming, pagination, response, authentication, and error handling patterns for a team.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Server Usefulness
use_cases.description: Create question-based evaluations that measure whether agents can complete realistic workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Plan My MCP Server
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new integration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder skill to plan an MCP server for [service]. Identify key workflows, tools, authentication needs, and transport choice.

prompt_templates.title: Design Tool Schemas
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder guidance to design MCP tools for [service API]. Include names, descriptions, inputs, outputs, pagination, and annotations.

prompt_templates.title: Review Implementation Quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Code review for an existing MCP server.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my MCP server against the mcp-builder checklists. Focus on validation, errors, auth, transport, output formats, and agent usability.

prompt_templates.title: Create Evaluation Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the mcp-builder evaluation process to create 10 read-only evaluation questions for this MCP server. Make answers stable, complex, and verifiable.

output_examples.input: Plan an MCP server for a ticketing system.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan with transport choice, authentication approach, core tools, schema notes, pagination rules, and test strategy.

output_examples.input: Review this MCP tool list for usability.
output: Improved error messages with next steps

output_examples.input: Create evaluation coverage for a GitHub MCP server.
output_examples.output: A set of realistic read-only questions with stable expected answers and guidance for running the evaluator.

best_practices: Use service-prefixed, action-oriented tool names with precise descriptions.
Validate every input with Zod or Pydantic and set clear size limits.
Create read-only evaluations that test real workflows before release.
anti_patterns: Do not expose raw API endpoints without agent-friendly workflow design.
Do not log secrets, tokens, headers, or full environment values.
Do not trust tool annotations as the only security control.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps design, implement, review, and evaluate MCP servers for external services.

faq.question: Does it support TypeScript and Python?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes TypeScript MCP SDK guidance and Python FastMCP guidance.

faq.question: Does it generate a full server automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns, examples, checklists, and evaluation scripts for guided implementation.

faq.question: Can it test an MCP server?
faq.answer: Yes. The included evaluator can test stdio, SSE, and streamable HTTP MCP servers.

faq.question: What credentials are needed?
faq.answer: The evaluator needs an Anthropic API key and may need service tokens for the MCP server under test.

faq.question: Is this safe for production work?
faq.answer: Use it with trusted target servers and review generated code before deployment.
